children.name: Agelaius
children.description: The red-winged and yellow-winged blackbirds — medium-sized icterids named for their distinctive shoulder patches. Males are black with bold red or yellow epaulets, which they flash aggressively during territorial displays. They are highly polygynous, with dominant males defending territories that attract multiple females, who build nests woven into cattails and marsh vegetation.

children.name: Agelaius phoeniceus
children.description: One of the most abundant and familiar birds in North America, found in virtually every marsh, wet meadow, and roadside ditch across the continent. The male is glossy black with brilliant red shoulder patches bordered by yellow; the female is heavily streaked brown, resembling a large sparrow. Its conk-la-ree! song and piercing alarm calls are defining sounds of North American wetlands.

children.name: Agelaius humeralis
children.description: A small blackbird with tawny-orange shoulder patches, found in Cuba and Haiti. It inhabits marshes, wet grasslands, and agricultural areas.

children.name: Agelaius xanthomus
children.description: A glossy black blackbird with bright yellow shoulder patches, endemic to Puerto Rico. It is classified as Endangered due to habitat loss and nest parasitism by cowbirds.

children.name: Agelaius tricolor
children.description: A colonial blackbird of California with a distinctive bright red shoulder patch bordered by white, unlike the yellow border of the Red-winged Blackbird. It forms enormous breeding colonies of thousands of birds in wetlands and agricultural fields. It is classified as Endangered due to habitat loss.

children.name: Agelaius assimilis
children.description: A small blackbird with red shoulder patches, endemic to Cuba. It inhabits marshes, wetlands, and rice fields across the island.

children.name: Quiscalus
children.description: The grackles — large, glossy blackbirds with long, keeled tails and pale yellow eyes. They are among the most intelligent of all songbirds, capable of solving complex puzzles and remembering individual human faces. Grackles are highly social, forming enormous noisy roosts, and have adapted brilliantly to urban and suburban environments.

children.name: Quiscalus quiscula
children.description: A large, iridescent blackbird with a long, keel-shaped tail and pale yellow eyes. Its glossy plumage shimmers with purple, bronze, and green in direct sunlight. It is a familiar bird across eastern North America, often seen in parking lots, agricultural fields, and suburban lawns where it forages with a distinctive stiff-legged walk.

children.name: Quiscalus mexicanus
children.description: A spectacularly long-tailed blackbird of the Americas, the male reaching up to 43 cm in length thanks to its oversized tail. It has undergone a dramatic range expansion northward in the last century, colonising much of the southern and central United States.

children.name: Quiscalus major
children.description: A large grackle restricted to salt and brackish marshes along the Atlantic and Gulf coasts of the United States. The male has a distinctive deep purplish-blue gloss and an enormously long, boat-shaped tail that gives the species its name. It is maritime in habit, nesting in marsh vegetation and foraging along shorelines.

children.name: Quiscalus nicaraguensis
children.description: A medium-sized grackle endemic to the wetlands of Lake Nicaragua and Lake Managua in Nicaragua. It has a keeled tail and yellow eyes, and is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range.

children.name: Quiscalus niger
children.description: A medium-sized grackle found throughout the Greater Antilles and the Cayman Islands. It has glossy black plumage with a purple sheen and a relatively short tail compared to mainland grackles.

children.name: Quiscalus lugubris
children.description: A small grackle found in northern South America, Trinidad and Tobago, and the Lesser Antilles. It has glossy black plumage with a purple sheen and a relatively short keeled tail.

children.name: Molothrus
children.description: The cowbirds — obligate brood parasites that lay their eggs in the nests of other passerine species. They are named for their historical association with bison herds, following the great herds to feed on insects flushed by their movement. A single female may lay several dozen eggs in a season, each in a different host nest, never building a nest or rearing her own young.

children.name: Molothrus ater
children.description: North America's most widespread brood parasite, known to have exploited over 200 host species. The male has a glossy black body with a brown head; the female is plain grey-brown. Historically it followed bison herds across the Great Plains, but deforestation and agriculture have vastly expanded its range.

children.name: Molothrus bonariensis
children.description: A brood parasite found across South America and expanding into the Caribbean and southeastern United States. The male is glossy blue-black and the female is dull grey-brown. It parasitises hundreds of host species.

children.name: Molothrus rufoaxillaris
children.description: A brood parasite found in eastern and southern South America, named for its piercing, high-pitched call. The male is glossy black with a chestnut wing patch visible in flight. It primarily parasitises the nests of other icterids.

children.name: Molothrus oryzivorus
children.description: The largest cowbird, resembling a grackle in size and appearance, found in lowland forests of Central and South America. It is a brood parasite that specialises in parasitising the nests of oropendolas and caciques.

children.name: Molothrus aeneus
children.description: A glossy black cowbird with a bronze sheen on the wings, found from the southwestern United States through Central America. It has a distinctively thick bill and red eyes. It parasitises a variety of host species.

children.name: Icterus
children.description: The New World orioles — brilliantly coloured blackbirds with a striking contrast of black and bright yellow, orange, or chestnut. They build remarkable hanging pouch nests woven from plant fibres, suspended from branch tips. Males sing rich, fluty whistles that carry through the treetops. They are primarily frugivorous and insectivorous, and many species undertake long migrations between breeding and wintering grounds.

children.name: Icterus galbula
children.description: The most familiar oriole of eastern North America, named for the black-and-orange colours of the Baltimore family coat of arms. The male flames with brilliant orange underparts and a rich black hood and back. It weaves an intricate hanging nest of plant fibres, hair, and string, suspended from elm, maple, and poplar branches.

children.name: Icterus spurius
children.description: The smallest North American oriole, the male is a rich chestnut-and-black unlike the orange of the Baltimore Oriole. It prefers open woodland, orchards, and farmland edges, building a hanging basket nest more loosely woven than that of its larger relative.

children.name: Icterus chrysocephalus
children.description: The Moriche Oriole is a striking black-and-yellow oriole with a bright yellow head, breast, and rump contrasting with black wings and tail. It is closely associated with Moriche palm groves in the Llanos and Amazon basin of South America.

children.name: Icterus cayanensis
children.description: The Epaulet Oriole is a slender black oriole with a yellow shoulder patch and yellow lower belly. It inhabits forests, savannas, and mangroves across northern and central South America.

children.name: Icterus pyrrhopterus
children.description: The Variable Oriole is a highly polymorphic species with black and yellow plumage varying geographically. It is found in a range of open and semi-open habitats across central South America.

children.name: Icterus leucopteryx
children.description: The Jamaican Oriole is a bright yellow and black oriole with white wing patches, endemic to Jamaica and the Cayman Islands. It inhabits forests and gardens, weaving its hanging nest in palm fronds.

children.name: Icterus graduacauda
children.description: Audubon's Oriole is a striking black-and-yellow oriole with a black hood, yellow body, and white wing bars. It inhabits woodlands and scrub of Mexico and southern Texas, where it is a rare and local resident.

children.name: Icterus pectoralis
children.description: A spectacular orange-and-black oriole with black spots on the breast, found from southern Mexico to Costa Rica. It has established an introduced population in southern Florida.

children.name: Icterus pustulatus
children.description: A bright orange oriole with black streaks on the back and a black throat, found from western Mexico to Costa Rica. It inhabits dry forests and scrub.

children.name: Icterus bullockii
children.description: A bright orange-and-black oriole of western North America, distinguished from the Baltimore Oriole by its orange cheeks and larger white wing patch. It builds a hanging nest in deciduous trees along rivers and in open woodlands.

children.name: Icterus cucullatus
children.description: A slender, bright orange oriole with a black throat and face, found from the southwestern United States to Mexico. It often nests in palm trees and is a common visitor to hummingbird feeders.

children.name: Icterus gularis
children.description: A large, bright yellow-and-black oriole with a massive bill, found from Mexico south to Nicaragua. It builds an extraordinarily long hanging nest, up to 70 cm in length, suspended from branches.

children.name: Icterus parisorum
children.description: A striking black-and-yellow oriole of the southwestern United States and Mexico, with a black head, back, and breast, and bright yellow underparts. It inhabits arid scrublands and juniper woodlands.

children.name: Icterus icterus
children.description: The national bird of Venezuela, a striking orange-and-black oriole with a white wing patch and a prominent blue eye-ring. It inhabits arid and semi-arid woodlands and is known for its melodious song.

children.name: Icterus jamacaii
children.description: A bright orange-oriole of northeastern Brazil, with a black hood and back and white wing patches. It inhabits the Caatinga and Cerrado regions.

children.name: Icterus croconotus
children.description: A striking orange-and-black oriole with an orange back, black hood, and white wing patch, found in southern Central America and much of South America east of the Andes.

children.name: Icterus abeillei
children.description: A striking black-and-yellow oriole with a black back, yellow underparts, and white wing patches, endemic to the Pacific slope of Mexico.

children.name: Icterus bonana
children.description: A black-and-orange oriole endemic to the island of Martinique in the Lesser Antilles, with a black head, orange body, and black wings. It is classified as Near Threatened.

children.name: Icterus laudabilis
children.description: A striking yellow-and-black oriole endemic to the island of Saint Lucia, with a yellow body, black hood, and black wings. It is classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Icterus oberi
children.description: A striking yellow-and-black oriole endemic to the island of Montserrat, with a yellow body, black hood, and black wings. It is classified as Critically Endangered due to volcanic eruptions and habitat loss.

children.name: Icterus portoricensis
children.description: A striking yellow-and-black oriole endemic to Puerto Rico, with a yellow body, black hood, and black wings. It inhabits forests and plantations.

children.name: Icterus dominicensis
children.description: A yellow-and-black oriole endemic to the island of Hispaniola, with a yellow body, black hood, and black wings. It is classified as Near Threatened.

children.name: Icterus wagleri
children.description: A black-and-yellow oriole with a black vent and tail, found from Mexico to Nicaragua. It inhabits forests and forest edges.

children.name: Icterus fuertesi
children.description: A bright orange-and-black oriole found in the lowlands of southern Mexico. It was formerly considered a subspecies of the Baltimore Oriole but has been split as a separate species.

children.name: Icterus auratus
children.description: A small, bright orange oriole with a black throat and face, found in the Yucatan Peninsula of Mexico and northern Central America. It inhabits dry forests and scrub.

children.name: Icterus mesomelas
children.description: A striking black-and-yellow oriole with a distinctive yellow tail, found from southern Mexico to western Ecuador. It inhabits humid forests and forest edges.

children.name: Icterus graceannae
children.description: A black-and-orange oriole with white edges to the wing feathers, found in the arid valleys of the Peruvian Andes.

children.name: Icterus maculialatus
children.description: A black-and-yellow oriole with white bars on the wings, found in the highlands of Chiapas, Mexico and Guatemala.

children.name: Icterus nigrogularis
children.description: A bright yellow oriole with a black throat and face, found in the Caribbean islands and northern South America. It inhabits open woodlands and gardens.

children.name: Icterus prosthemelas
children.description: A black-and-yellow oriole with a black hood extending to the breast, found from southern Mexico to Panama.

children.name: Icterus chrysater
children.description: A black-and-yellow oriole with a yellow back and black head and throat, found from southern Mexico to Colombia.

children.name: Xanthocephalus
children.description: The yellow-headed blackbird — a striking monotypic genus named for the male's vivid yellow head and breast. It breeds in the marshes of western and central North America, often alongside Red-winged Blackbirds. Males defend territories in deep-water cattail marshes and perform impressive flight displays, singing a grating, strained song while hanging from cattail stems.

children.name: Xanthocephalus xanthocephalus
children.description: One of the most striking American songbirds: the male has a brilliant yellow hood and breast contrasting with a jet-black body and white wing patches. It breeds in large marshes across western and central North America, where its harsh, croaking song and metallic calls carry across the water.

children.name: Dolichonyx
children.description: The bobolink — a monotypic genus of Grassland icterid with one of the longest migrations of any passerine. The male's breeding plumage is unique among North American birds: black below and on the head, with a buff nape and white back and rump. The species is named for its bubbling, ecstatic song, which pours forth in sustained, tumbling phrases from the male's flight display over hayfields and prairies.

children.name: Dolichonyx oryzivorus
children.description: A remarkable grassland bird that undertakes one of the longest migrations of any New World passerine — up to 20,000 km round trip from the prairies of North America to the pampas of Argentina. The male in breeding plumage is striking: black underparts and head, buff nape, white back and rump.

children.name: Sturnella
children.description: The meadowlarks — stocky, ground-dwelling icterids with bright yellow underparts and a bold black V on the breast. Despite their name they are not larks but true blackbirds, and are among the most iconic birds of American grasslands. Their clear, fluting songs carry across open fields, and they are more often heard than seen, perching on fence posts and low trees to deliver their melodies.

children.name: Sturnella magna
children.description: The eastern counterpart of the grassland meadowlark pair, with a clear, plaintive song of two to five whistled notes, the last descending. It inhabits pastures, meadows, and prairies across eastern North America, Central America, and into northern South America.

children.name: Sturnella neglecta
children.description: Nearly identical in appearance to the Eastern Meadowlark but told apart by its song: a rich, fluting, seven-note warble that descends the scale in a sweet, bubbling phrase. The state bird of six US states, it is a symbol of the American prairie.

children.name: Sturnella militaris
children.description: The Red-breasted Blackbird has a striking black body with a vivid red breast, throat, and shoulder patch. It inhabits wet grasslands, marshes, and rice fields across much of South America east of the Andes.

children.name: Sturnella superciliaris
children.description: The White-browed Blackbird has a black body with a red breast and a distinctive white supercilium and cheek stripe. It is common in grasslands and agricultural areas of eastern and southern South America.

children.name: Leistes
children.description: A genus of South American meadowlarks separated from Sturnella by genetic evidence, with bright red or yellow underparts and a strong preference for grasslands. Males are highly territorial and perform conspicuous flight displays.

children.name: Leistes loyca
children.description: A large meadowlark with a bright red throat and breast, black body, and a long tail, found in the southern cone of South America. It inhabits grasslands and pastures.

children.name: Leistes bellicosus
children.description: A striking black-and-red meadowlark found in the coastal lowlands of Peru and Ecuador. It inhabits irrigated grasslands and agricultural areas.

children.name: Leistes defilippii
children.description: A black-and-red meadowlark endemic to the grasslands of Argentina and Uruguay. It has declined significantly due to agricultural intensification and is classified as Near Threatened.

children.name: Euphagus
children.description: The American blackbirds — a small genus of two species that bridge the gap between grackles and true blackbirds. They are medium-sized icterids with glossy black plumage, pale eyes, and relatively slender bills. One species is a common bird of towns and farms, while the other has suffered a dramatic and poorly understood population decline.

children.name: Euphagus cyanocephalus
children.description: A common and adaptable blackbird of western and central North America, often found in parking lots, parks, and agricultural areas where it forages on the ground with a jaunty walk. The male is glossy black with a purple-green iridescence and a pale yellow eye; the female is plain grey-brown.

children.name: Euphagus carolinus
children.description: A boreal forest blackbird that has experienced one of the most severe population declines of any North American bird — a 90% reduction since the 1960s for reasons that remain unclear. In winter, its glossy black feathers acquire rusty-brown tips, giving the species its common name.

children.name: Cacicus
children.description: The caciques — medium-sized icterids with predominantly black plumage accented by bright yellow, red, or orange patches. They are social birds that build long, hanging pouch nests in colonies, often near wasp nests for protection from predators.

children.name: Cacicus cela
children.description: A glossy black bird with a bright yellow rump, tail base, and wing patch, and a pale yellow bill. It is a highly social species that nests colonially in trees, weaving long hanging pouch nests.

children.name: Cacicus haemorrhous
children.description: A glossy black bird with a vivid crimson rump and lower belly. It is a colonial nester found in lowland rainforests of the Amazon and Orinoco basins.

children.name: Cacicus solitarius
children.description: A glossy black bird with a pale yellow bill and a solitary or pair-living habit, unlike most other caciques. It inhabits riparian forests and swamps of central South America.

children.name: Cacicus koepckeae
children.description: A glossy black cacique with a yellow rump and tail base, found in the lowland forests of southeastern Peru and adjacent Bolivia. It is poorly known.

children.name: Cacicus sclateri
children.description: A glossy black cacique with a yellow rump and tail, found in the lowland forests of eastern Ecuador and Peru. It was named after British zoologist Philip Sclater.

children.name: Cacicus chrysonotus
children.description: A striking black cacique with a bright yellow rump and a yellow-tipped tail, found in the montane forests of the Andes from Colombia to Bolivia. It inhabits high-elevation cloud forests.

children.name: Cacicus chrysopterus
children.description: A small black cacique with distinctive golden-yellow wing patches, found in the lowland forests of the Amazon basin. It inhabits forest edges and clearings.

children.name: Psarocolius
children.description: The oropendolas — large icterids with striking combination of black, chestnut, and yellow plumage, and long, pointed bills with colourful tips. They are colonial breeders that weave extraordinary hanging nests up to two metres long, suspended from isolated trees.

children.name: Psarocolius decumanus
children.description: A large blackbird with a black body, chestnut lower back and rump, bright yellow tail, and a long, slender crest. It builds hanging nests up to 1.5 metres long in colonies across South America.

children.name: Psarocolius viridis
children.description: An olive-green bird with a bright yellow tail and a pale greenish bill with a red tip. It inhabits lowland rainforests of the Amazon and Guiana Shield regions.

children.name: Psarocolius angustifrons
children.description: A bird with a rich chestnut back, black head and underparts, and a yellow tail with black central feathers. It is found in montane and lowland forests of the northern Andes and Amazon basin.

children.name: Psarocolius bifasciatus
children.description: A large olive-brown oropendola with a yellow tail and a bare pink facial patch, found in the lowland rainforests of the Amazon basin.

children.name: Psarocolius atrovirens
children.description: A dark olive-green oropendola with a yellow tail and a pale bill, found in the montane forests of the eastern Andes from Peru to Bolivia.

children.name: Psarocolius wagleri
children.description: A striking oropendola with a chestnut head, black body, and yellow tail, found from southern Mexico to northwestern South America. It builds long hanging nests in colonies.

children.name: Psarocolius montezuma
children.description: A spectacular chestnut-and-black oropendola with a bright yellow tail and a blue face patch, found from southern Mexico to Panama. Males have a distinctive gurgling song and perform elaborate bowing displays.

children.name: Psarocolius guatimozinus
children.description: A glossy black oropendola with a yellow tail and a pale yellow bill, found in the lowland forests of the Choco region of Colombia and Panama.

children.name: Psarocolius cassini
children.description: A black oropendola with a chestnut lower back and a yellow tail, found in the lowland forests of the Choco region of Colombia.

children.name: Gymnomystax
children.description: The oriole blackbird — a monotypic genus of striking black-and-yellow icterid found in South America. Despite its name, it is not a true oriole but a blackbird with a similar colour pattern.

children.name: Gymnomystax mexicanus
children.description: A striking black-and-yellow icterid with a black head, throat, back, and wings, and bright yellow underparts and shoulders, found in the lowlands of northern South America. It inhabits savannas, river edges, and marshes.

children.name: Dives
children.description: A genus of medium-sized, entirely black icterids with a strong, musical song and a preference for open habitats. They are the most thrush-like in appearance among the blackbirds.

children.name: Dives dives
children.description: A medium-sized, all-black icterid with a long tail and a rich, melodious song, found from southern Mexico to Costa Rica. It inhabits open woodlands, forest edges, and gardens.

children.name: Dives warczewiczi
children.description: A medium-sized, all-black icterid found in the arid scrub of Peru and Ecuador. It inhabits coastal deserts and dry valleys of the Andes.

children.name: Amblycercus holosericeus
children.description: A glossy black bird with a striking pale yellow or ivory bill, found from Mexico to Bolivia.

children.name: Chrysomus ruficapillus
children.description: A blackbird with a chestnut cap and throat, found in marshes and wet grasslands of southeastern South America.

children.name: Chrysomus icterocephalus
children.description: A striking blackbird with a bright yellow head, neck, and breast, found in marshes of northern South America.

children.name: Pseudoleistes guirahuro
children.description: A striking black bird with a bright yellow rump and lower breast, found in marshes and wet grasslands of central South America.

children.name: Pseudoleistes virescens
children.description: A brown bird with a yellow belly and shoulder, found in marshes of southeastern South America.

children.name: Curaeus curaeus
children.description: A medium-sized all-black icterid found in the southern forests and scrub of Chile and Argentina.

children.name: Nesopsar nigerrimus
children.description: A small all-black icterid endemic to Jamaica, with a narrow pointed bill. Endangered due to habitat loss.

children.name: Hypopyrrhus pyrohypogaster
children.description: A glossy black grackle with a bright red belly, endemic to the Colombian Andes. Vulnerable.

children.name: Lampropsar tanagrinus
children.description: A small silky-black grackle with a distinctive velvet-like forehead patch, found in the Amazon basin.

children.name: Gnorimopsar chopi
children.description: A glossy black icterid with a stout bill and a distinctive song, found in savannas and open woodlands of central South America.

children.name: Oreopsar bolivianus
children.description: A medium-sized all-black icterid endemic to the intermontane valleys of Bolivia.

children.name: Agelaioides badius
children.description: A brownish icterid with chestnut wing patches, found in open woodlands of southern South America. Unlike true cowbirds, it is not an obligate brood parasite.

children.name: Amblyramphus holosericeus
children.description: A striking black bird with a brilliant scarlet-red head, throat, and upper breast, found in marshes of southern South America.

children.name: Anumara forbesi
children.description: A medium-sized all-black icterid endemic to northeastern Brazil. Endangered due to habitat loss.

children.name: Xanthopsar flavus
children.description: A brilliant yellow-and-black icterid found in marshes and wet grasslands of southern South America. Vulnerable.
